bailey_yi.name: Bailey Yi
bailey_yi.summary:
Out of Indiana, Bailey Yi is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U13 level, with 4 entries across 2 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

Bailey is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.
bailey_yi.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Bailey Yi, from Indiana, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 4 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Bailey is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 MBC Midwest OLC, and so far, the only thing Bailey has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Bailey saga began at 2024 MBC Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Bailey plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 4 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Bailey has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Bailey has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Bailey showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13, which means Bailey traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Bailey entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Bailey paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Bailey is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Bailey wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Bailey, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

bala_sabarish_venkatakrishnan.name: Bala Sabarish Venkatakrishnan
bala_sabarish_venkatakrishnan.summary:
A Michigan competitor, Bala Sabarish Venkatakrishnan has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U13 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 8 entries in 3 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Notably, Bala has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Bala competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Bala has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Rajguru Reddy Are (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)

Bala has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
bala_sabarish_venkatakrishnan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Bala Sabarish Venkatakrishnan, from Michigan, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 8 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Bala is right on schedule. It all started at 2023 Egret Midwest OLC, and so far, the only thing Bala has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Bala saga began at 2023 Egret Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Bala plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 8 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Bala has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Bala has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Bala showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13, which means Bala traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Bala entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Bala paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Bala keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Bala has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Bala, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Bala. We will be here, taking notes.

bavish_reddi_dornala.name: Bavish Reddi Dornala
bavish_reddi_dornala.summary:
Out of Illinois, Bavish Reddi Dornala has collected 2 tournament titles, currently competing at the U17 level, capturing 2 titles across 14 tournaments and 35 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Bavish keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Notably, Bavish has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Bavish is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

Bavish has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 7 different players across the career:
  • Nithesh Madishetti (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (7 events, best: #4, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Sunny Zheng (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (4 total events in BD, across 2 seasons)
  • Catherine Du (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in XD

Bavish is peaking at the right time in 2025-2026, with 2 titles across 4 tournaments — a clear sign that Bavish is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Bavish has competed across U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Bavish isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 2 titles, Bavish is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
bavish_reddi_dornala.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Bavish Reddi Dornala, from Illinois, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Bavish has 35 career entries across 14 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 6% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Bavish saga began at 2023 Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2025 Egret Midwest OLC in BD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Bavish's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 2 titles in 10 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Bavish plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Bavish is 0-for-14. Zero titles in 14 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Bavish and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Bavish needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Bavish has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Nithesh Madishetti: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Nithesh Madishetti has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Ayaana Makker has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Bavish's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Bavish has shown up 3 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Bavish has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Bavish has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Bavish has 18 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Bavish showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13, which means Bavish traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Bavish entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Bavish paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 35 entries, and 33 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Bavish looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Bavish's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Bavish has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Bavish. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

bella_liu.name: Bella Liu
bella_liu.summary:
Out of California, Bella Liu is a Pan American Junior Championships silver medalist, earning a podium finish representing the USA against the best young players in the Western Hemisphere (XD U13, GD U13). A top-2 finish at the Pan Am Juniors puts Bella in rarified international company.

Bella is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (5 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

bella_liu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Bella Liu, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Bella is a Pan American Junior Championships silver medalist (XD U13, GD U13), which means Bella has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Bella's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. But here is the thing about a silver medal: it means Bella went all the way to an international championship and STILL did not win. Bella represented an entire country and came home with the consolation prize. Across 76 career entries and a 20% win rate, the Pan Am silver is the crown jewel of a résumé that is otherwise... well, let's just say we have 76 entries worth of material.

The Bella saga began at 2022 SoCal Arena CRC, where Bella burst onto the scene with a title in XD U11 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Bella has delivered on some of those promises: 15 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Bella promised and what Bella has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 7 titles in 27 entries — a stretch where Bella actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 2 wins in 12 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Bella experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Bella has reached 9 championship matches across this career. That is 9 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 9 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 Pan Am Junior, where Bella entered 4 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U13, XD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (6 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Bella does own 15 actual titles, so it is not like Bella has never won anything. But 9 finals losses versus 15 wins means Bella is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Bella plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 6 doubles, 4 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Bella can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 18 of them, to be precise. Bella has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Bella's texts. The most successful partnership is with Sophia Liu — 10 events, 4 titles — and honestly, Sophia Liu deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Sophia Liu a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U13 — we hope Enming Cao has emotionally recovered.

Tournament committees have handed Bella a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Bella has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2023 SoCal ORC in GS U11, where Bella was seeded #2 and finished 3rd. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

One thing nobody can accuse Bella of is playing it safe. Bella has entered older age groups at 5 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15 — which means Bella has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 12 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Bella has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Bella is not just losing locally — Bella is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 20% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Liu family travels cross-country so Bella can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Bella has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Bella showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in GS U11, which means Bella traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 South ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Bella entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Bella paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 15 titles to the name, 76 entries, and 61 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Pan Am medal, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Bella looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Bella's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Bella has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Bella. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

bella_wang.roast:

Bella is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Bella's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Bella is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

Bella is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Bella wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Bella, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

benjamin_dictado.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Benjamin Dictado, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. And yes, Benjamin IS a Junior National Champion — BD U19 — which is a legitimate, no-asterisks, put-it-on-the-college-application achievement. We will give that its due respect for about five seconds... okay, time's up. Because behind those headlines is a 52-entry career across 18 tournaments with a 15% win rate, which means 44 times — 44! — Benjamin went home without a trophy. So buckle in, because we have 52 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Benjamin saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2022 Dave Freeman OLC in BS U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Benjamin's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Benjamin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 4 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Benjamin can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Benjamin has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Benjamin's texts. The go-to partner is Bettie Huang (5 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-25 exit at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U17 — we hope Brianna Mia Xu has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Benjamin entered 9 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Benjamin's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Benjamin and said 'nah.' Tournament committees have handed Benjamin a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Benjamin has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2022 NW ORC in BS U17, where Benjamin was seeded #1 and finished 3rd. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

Benjamin has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Benjamin is not just losing locally — Benjamin is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 15% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Dictado family travels cross-country so Benjamin can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Benjamin has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Benjamin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U19, which means Benjamin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 U.S. Selection Event in U19 BS. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Selection Event, where Benjamin entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 19th, which is a polite way of saying Benjamin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 8 titles to the name, 52 entries, and 44 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Benjamin looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Benjamin's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Benjamin has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Benjamin. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

bingru_huang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Bingru Huang, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 12 events across 4 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 12-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Bingru's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Bingru keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Bingru saga began at 2025 CAN-AM Elite NorCal OLC with 3 entries and a best of 3rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Bingru plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 12 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Bingru has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Bingru's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in XD U13 — we hope Nathan Jeremy Santoso has emotionally recovered.

Bingru has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Bingru has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Bingru showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in GS U13, which means Bingru traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Bingru entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Bingru paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Bingru keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Bingru has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Bingru, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Bingru. We will be here, taking notes.

bivab_chandra.name: Bivab Chandra
bivab_chandra.summary:
A Georgia competitor, Bivab Chandra has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U15 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Bivab's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

What makes Bivab especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

With 22 different doubles partners over the years, Bivab adapts to anyone:
  • Roger Kung (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)

Bivab has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Bivab has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 5 seasons and 1 title, Bivab has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
bivab_chandra.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Bivab Chandra, from Georgia, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-33. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Bivab has 50 career entries across 19 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 2% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Bivab saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2024 South OLC (Nets & Turf) in BD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Bivab's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 1 title in 17 entries — a stretch where Bivab actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Bivab is 0-for-8. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Bivab experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Bivab has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Bivab does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Bivab has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 1 wins means Bivab is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Bivab plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Bivab is 0-for-19. Zero titles in 19 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Bivab and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Bivab needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 22 of them, to be precise. Bivab has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Bivab's texts. The go-to partner is Yashas Cheerlavancha (5 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U13 — we hope Yashas Cheerlavancha has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Bivab's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Bivab has shown up 2 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Bivab is not just losing locally — Bivab is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chandra family travels cross-country so Bivab can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Bivab has 28 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Bivab showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13, which means Bivab traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Bivab entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Bivab paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 50 entries, and 49 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Bivab looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Bivab's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Bivab has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Bivab. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

boqian_zheng.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Boqian Zheng, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is a player who has stood at the top of the podium at Junior Nationals — the crown jewel of the American junior badminton calendar — and won BD U11. That is a real, verified, indisputable accomplishment, and no one can ever take it away. But here's what they CAN take away: any illusion that the rest of this resume is equally impressive. Because we are looking at 50 career entries across 18 tournaments, 3 total wins, and 47 losses. A 6% win rate that suggests the Nationals title was less a coronation and more a cosmic accident that Boqian has been unsuccessfully trying to replicate ever since. The story started at 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC), and it has been a wild, inconsistent, deeply roastable ride from there.

The Boqian saga began at 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC) with 2 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Boqian's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 3 titles in 22 entries — a stretch where Boqian actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Boqian is 0-for-15. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Boqian plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Boqian is 0-for-18. Zero titles in 18 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Boqian and suddenly the wins start appearing — 3 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Boqian needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Boqian has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Boqian's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Aadya Yanamandra: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Aadya Yanamandra has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Naksh Shetty has emotionally recovered.

Boqian has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Boqian is not just losing locally — Boqian is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zheng family travels cross-country so Boqian can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Boqian has 16 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Boqian showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BS U13, which means Boqian traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Boqian entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 65th. That is an average placement of 34th, which is a polite way of saying Boqian paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 50 entries, and 47 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Boqian looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Boqian's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Boqian has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Boqian. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Bowen has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Bowen's texts. The go-to partner is Kylie Ou (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Bowen's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 35 entries, and 34 losses. brandon_xu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Brandon Xu, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Brandon is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist (BD U15, BS U15, XD U15), which means Brandon has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Brandon's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. A Pan Am gold medal. International champion. The real deal. We would love to end the story right here, but unfortunately there are 6 career entries to discuss and a 83% overall win rate that suggests the Pan Am gold was the peak of a very pointy mountain with a LOT of valley on either side. Let's dig in.

The Brandon saga began at 2017 Pan Am Junior, where Brandon burst onto the scene with a title in BS U15 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Brandon has delivered on some of those promises: 5 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Brandon promised and what Brandon has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2016-2017 season — 5 titles in 5 entries — a stretch where Brandon actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Brandon is 0-for-1. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Brandon plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Brandon can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Brandon has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Brandon showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Brandon traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 5 titles to the name, 6 entries, and 1 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Pan Am medal, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Brandon looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Brandon's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Brandon has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Brandon. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Take 2022 Egret Midwest CRC, where Brendan entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U15, BD U15). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (7 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Brendan does own 7 actual titles, so it is not like Brendan has never won anything. But 10 finals losses versus 7 wins means Brendan is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Brendan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Brendan is 0-for-24. Zero titles in 24 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Brendan and suddenly the wins start appearing — 7 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Brendan needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 22 of them, to be precise. Brendan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Brendan's texts. The go-to partner is Ishi Reddy (6 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-97 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Selina Xu has emotionally recovered.

Of Brendan's 7 career titles, 4 came at local events versus 3 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Brendan feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Brendan entered 3 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Brendan's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Brendan and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Brendan has shown up 9 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7. Tournament committees have handed Brendan a #1 or #2 seed 4 times, and Brendan has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2024 MBC Midwest OLC in BS U19, where Brendan was seeded #2 and finished 5th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

Brendan has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Brendan is not just losing locally — Brendan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 9% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chen family travels cross-country so Brendan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-97 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Brendan has 18 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Brendan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 97th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15, which means Brendan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 SoCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Brendan entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 97th. That is an average placement of 41st, which is a polite way of saying Brendan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 7 titles to the name, 78 entries, and 71 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Brendan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Brendan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Brendan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Brendan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Brianna has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 14 different players across the career:
  • Micah Cruz (current primary partner) — 6 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (7 total events in GD/U15, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Vivek Rai (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U15/XD (3 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Nihal Kadamba (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

Brianna has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Brianna has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 4 seasons and 7 titles, Brianna has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
brianna_lee.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Brianna Lee, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Brianna is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Brianna has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Brianna would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Brianna, we kept reading — all 48 entries across 17 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 15% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Brianna held up a trophy, there were roughly 5 other times Brianna walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Brianna saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in GS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Brianna's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 4 titles in 20 entries — a stretch where Brianna actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 8 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Brianna experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Brianna has reached 6 championship matches across this career. That is 6 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 6 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Brianna does own 7 actual titles, so it is not like Brianna has never won anything. But 6 finals losses versus 7 wins means Brianna is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Brianna plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 2 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Brianna can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Brianna has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Brianna's texts. The go-to partner is Micah Cruz (7 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U15 — we hope Max Zhang has emotionally recovered.

Tournament committees have handed Brianna a #1 or #2 seed 6 times, and Brianna has failed to live up to it in 2 of them — including at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in GS U17, where Brianna was seeded #2 and finished 5th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

One thing nobody can accuse Brianna of is playing it safe. Brianna has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17 — which means Brianna has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 7 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Brianna has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Brianna is not just losing locally — Brianna is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 15% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lee family travels cross-country so Brianna can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Brianna has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Brianna showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U15, which means Brianna traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 NorCal CRC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 7 titles to the name, 48 entries, and 41 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Brianna looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Brianna's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Brianna has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Brianna. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

brianna_mia_xu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Brianna Mia Xu, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 20 career entries in 10 tournaments, Brianna has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 19 entries where Brianna drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 20 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Brianna saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Brianna plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 20 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Brianna has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-25 exit at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U17 — we hope Benjamin Dictado has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Brianna entered 6 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Brianna's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Brianna and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Brianna has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Brianna has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Brianna has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Brianna has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Brianna showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 25th-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U17, which means Brianna traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-25 at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Brianna entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 25th. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Brianna paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 20 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Brianna. Brianna just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Brianna has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Brianna. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Brianna: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Vivaan Khullar has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Catherine Du has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Brighton has shown up 6 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Brighton has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Brighton has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Brighton has 27 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Brighton showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U13, which means Brighton traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Brighton entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Brighton paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 51 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Brighton. Brighton just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Brighton has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Brighton. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Brighton: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.
